behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at a module handoff boundary (request builder -> API client). A unit test of a helper string builder could miss wiring bugs where labels are dropped before dispatch. Real OS/network/LLM behavior is not required; a stub transport that captures outbound payload is sufficient. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is about request shape, not live HTTP behavior or model decision quality.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the classifier module with a fake API client that records requests. Invoke classify() with a taxonomy, then assert the outbound payload contains every configured label exactly as required.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in response-handling across modules (API adapter response -> taxonomy validator -> public return). A single-function unit test could miss schema/adapter wiring mistakes. No real OS/network or model-quality dependence is needed; deterministic stub responses reproduce this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use a fake API client returning an out-of-taxonomy label (for example "medical"). Call classify() and assert returned value is exactly "unknown".

behaviors.description: For identical raw API responses, the module's normalization step always produces the same final label.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is explicitly about deterministic normalization logic for identical inputs; this is a local function/object boundary with no required I/O. Escalation rejected: no cross-module handoff is required by the behavior text, and no real infrastructure or model output variation is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Call normalization with the same raw response payload multiple times and assert the exact same normalized label each time.

behaviors.description: On repeated live API calls for the same document and taxonomy, the returned label remains consistent unless the provider response itself changes.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly requires repeated live AI API calls and checks consistency relative to provider outputs. A mock would hide real model/provider variability, which is the failure boundary. Not Workflow: this is repeated single-step classification calls, not a multi-step agent pipeline with chained model decisions.
behaviors.test_description: Run N real classification calls for the same input against the provider, log raw provider responses and final labels, and assert final-label changes only occur when raw provider output meaningfully changes.

behaviors.description: Request construction enforces the configured token budget before dispatch (for example truncate/reject according to policy).
behaviors.justification: This is orchestration across tokenizer/budget policy and dispatch control (possibly "do not send" path). A pure unit test of truncation alone can miss dispatch gating failures. Real network or model output is unnecessary; in-process fakes can verify both transformed payload and whether dispatch was blocked.
behaviors.test_description: Configure a small token budget and a fake API client. Submit oversized input and assert policy result (truncate or reject) and that dispatch behavior matches policy (sent truncated request or no call).

behaviors.description: Provider-reported token usage for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: Catching this failure requires real provider accounting, which occurs on a real AI API call. Stubs can only mirror expected numbers and may miss true tokenizer/accounting drift at the provider boundary. Rejected Workflow: behavior is a single-call budget check, not multiple agentic stages in sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Execute real API calls for representative documents, capture provider-reported token usage metadata, and assert usage is <= configured per-call budget.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: Primary failure is retry orchestration (error mapping -> wait/backoff -> retry -> return) across local modules. This can be reproduced with a deterministic fake API and fake clock; real network/OS behavior is not required for the core correctness claim. Rejected higher levels: behavior text does not require real provider timing quirks or model-output-quality checks.
behaviors.test_description: Use a fake API client that returns rate-limit error first and success on retry; use controllable clock/timer. Assert module waits per policy, retries, and returns successful label instead of surfacing error.
